input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle that has been retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology. Its hardware consists of a lower‑level chassis, an upper‑level chassis, and integrated autonomy elements (e.g., perception sensors, actuators). The vehicle operates in the automotive Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain, where on‑board electronic control units (ECUs) are resource‑constrained, making it an appropriate testbed for off‑loading computation to a digital twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a user interface (UI) displays real‑time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and detected object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of these data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation) is executed on the DT (e.g., in the cloud) and the resulting desired speed is sent back to the vehicle for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators to remotely enable/disable ACC, set a desired speed, and issue an emergency‑braking command, all over a low end‑to‑end latency link. The services support operators (monitoring and management) and developers (troubleshooting via data collection).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation follows the ISO 23247‑1:2021 Digital Twin framework, using its entity‑based reference architecture. The system comprises a Data Collection and Device Control Entity that gathers sensor data, a Digital Twin Entity that models the physical vehicle, and a User Entity that provides the UI. Functional Entities within each entity perform tasks such as data transformation and actuation. The architecture may be realized with standards‑based middleware such as OPC Unified Architecture. A publicly available reference implementation is hosted at https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin, and a demonstration video is linked in the documentation. Cloud computing resources are used for the off‑loaded ACC computation.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires a shared notion of time across device components to enable computation offloading, with a clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms as mandated by safety‑critical vehicular constraints; this synchronization supports the low‑latency data exchange needed for the ACC offloading and remote management services.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The DTS is built from three top‑level entities defined by ISO 23247 – Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity – each instantiated once and deployed on separate hardware resources; within each entity a set of functional entities (FEs) is realized as ROS 2 packages containing one or more nodes, yielding a hierarchical, decentralized structure rather than multiple twin instances.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestration follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture with an entity‑based pattern; inter‑entity communication and coordination are realized through ROS 2 middleware (packages and nodes) and a network link that provides low end‑to‑end latency between the vehicle (physical twin) and the remote DT where ACC computation is offloaded; the DT Entity aggregates data, performs processing, and returns control commands to the Physical Twin Entity, while the User Entity provides UI interaction.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems is contemplated through standard data‑exchange mechanisms such as OPC Unified Architecture, as suggested by the ISO 23247 specification, and via ROS 2 message transport; the UI communicates with the DT over the same low‑latency network, enabling remote monitoring and management, though specific external enterprise system bindings are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin System (DTS) implements a behavioral Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) model that processes sensor inputs to identify the lead vehicle, computes a desired speed, and returns this speed to the physical vehicle for actuation. The ACC model is executed in the Digital Twin Entity after being off‑loaded from the on‑board ECU. Input data originate from the physical twin’s sensors (vehicle state, position, velocity, and relative distance to detected objects). In addition, the DTS hosts a point‑cloud clustering and tracking module (used for object detection and projected driving path) within the Physical Twin Entity. All collected data are managed by the Data Storage component and made available to the User Interface for real‑time monitoring, logging and report generation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed on the physical vehicle by applying the desired speed computed by the off‑loaded ACC model. Control commands—including enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and emergency braking—are issued from the User Interface (User Entity) and transmitted to the vehicle via the Data Collection and Device Control Entity. The commands address the vehicle’s throttle and brake actuators through the vehicle’s command interface. Safety‑critical operation observes a 100 ms clock‑synchronisation accuracy requirement for command delivery, as mandated for vehicular applications.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The physical twin consists of a 1/10‑scale autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensing hardware. Sensors are mounted on the lower and upper chassis and on the autonomy elements, providing real‑time measurements of vehicle state, object positions, and relative distances. The sensor suite generates point‑cloud data that feed the clustering and tracking module. Exact sensor types, sampling frequencies, or data transmission rates are not specified in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point‑cloud, motor status, steering servo position, and overall vehicle state) is streamed to the Digital Twin System (DTS) via a Wi‑Fi link that uses UDP as the transport protocol. Within the DTS, the raw LiDAR points are first processed by the *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking* software component (implemented as a ROS2 node in the Physical Twin Entity). All incoming streams then pass through the ISO 23247 *Data Translation* functional element (Data Translation FE), which serialises each message, aligns the message types to the DTS internal schema and performs required unit conversions. The processed data are published on a ROS2 network that interconnects all software components of the DTS. The flow is triggered continuously in real‑time, with the purpose of supporting Data Monitoring (real‑time display of vehicle state, object tracks and projected driving path). Data quality assurance is achieved by the preprocessing step (clustering & tracking) and by the Data Translation FE’s type‑checking and unit‑conversion logic before the data are made available to higher‑level services.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands originate in the User Entity’s Control Panel (part of the User Interface FE) and are sent to the Physical Twin Entity over the same Wi‑Fi/UDP link used for sensor data. Commands include *enable/disable ACC*, *set desired speed*, *emergency braking*, and the *desired speed* value computed by the off‑loaded ACC service. Each command is encapsulated as a ROS2 message, passed through the Data Translation FE for format validation and unit conversion, and then delivered to the *Actuation FE* which drives the motor and steering servo actuators. Feedback is provided by the vehicle’s state sensors, which are streamed back to the DTS, allowing the UI to confirm command execution. Although explicit authentication mechanisms are not described, the architecture relies on the Data Translation FE to validate message structure before actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DTS uses a hybrid network architecture: intra‑entity communication between ROS2 components runs on a ROS2 middleware bus, while inter‑entity communication (between Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity and User Entity) is carried out over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP at the transport layer. The system requires a maximum end‑to‑end latency that supports real‑time control and monitoring; a synchronization accuracy of 100 ms is mandated for safety‑critical coordination. No explicit bandwidth figures are given, but the use of UDP implies a design for low‑latency, best‑effort delivery. Reliability and redundancy mechanisms, as well as security protocols (e.g., TLS, authentication), are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The ACC functionality is off‑loaded from the on‑board ECU to a remote computing platform referred to as the cloud; this constitutes the primary hosting environment for the Digital Twin. The cloud host runs ROS2 packages that implement the ACC service, Data Translation, Data Storage and UI services. Computational requirements include sufficient processing power to perform lead‑vehicle detection, speed‑setpoint calculation and real‑time data aggregation, while storage must accommodate collected vehicle‑state logs and generated reports. The architecture supports scaling by deploying additional ROS2 nodes on the cloud as needed, but explicit scaling mechanisms are not described. Deployment models are limited to cloud‑based execution for the off‑loaded ACC service; edge or on‑premise deployment is not mentioned. Availability is addressed only implicitly via the requirement for low latency and continuous data streaming.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture. First, key software components are identified (see Table I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators are defined. Each component is then mapped to a Functional Entity (FE) and implemented as an individual ROS 2 package; every package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that realize the FE’s functionality. The mapping respects the entity‑based structure of ISO 23247, placing user‑facing components in the User Entity, data‑management and control mechanisms in the Digital Twin Entity, and data pre‑processing, actuation and device communication in the Physical Twin Entity. Deployment is performed on separate hardware units, allowing flexible placement of FEs. The case study was carried out by two graduate students as a course project, but the documents do not detail explicit quality‑assurance, testing, version‑control, or evolution‑management practices.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system is built around ISO 23247 (Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing), specifically leveraging Part 2 (Reference Architecture) to structure entities and functional entities. The architecture is deliberately independent of specific data formats or communication protocols, allowing integration of domain‑specific technologies such as ROS 2. To address safety‑critical automotive requirements, the authors recommend complementing ISO 23247 with ISO 26262 (road vehicles – functional safety) and aligning the deployment model with AUTOSAR, the de‑facto automotive software architecture standard, which provides hardware‑agnostic component definition and supports retro‑fitting existing automotive platforms.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The automotive digital‑twin case study notes that the ISO 23247 reference architecture does not address safety‑critical requirements; therefore the authors recommend elevating safety to a first‑class concern by incorporating the functional‑safety standard ISO 26262 (Road vehicles – functional safety). This integration implies applying ISO 26262 safety‑lifecycle activities—hazard analysis, risk assessment, allocation of safety goals, and implementation of fail‑safe mechanisms—within the DT, for example for the Data Pre‑Processing and Actuation functional entities deployed on the physical twin.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

V. R EFLECTION AND C ONCLUSIONS


We reported on a case study of an automotive DT, through
which we investigate the fitness of the ISO 23247 standard
in engineering such systems. Our experiences highlight both
benefits and limitations of the standard in automotive settings.
On a positive note, we found the standard’s reference
architecture (RA) to be **a useful starting point for auto-**
**motive DTs** as it provides guidance for organizing blocks
of functionality. Additionally, we found the standard **easy to**
**use** as it does not assume substantial DT expertise and has a
comfortable learning curve. Our case was developed by two
graduate students, starting from a course project. [4] Through
this case study, we also highlight the **convenient generality**
of ISO 23247. The independence from specific format and
protocol requirements, as well as the ability to rely on domainspecific technologies in a straightforward manner, facilitated
the transfer of the RA to a new domain (automotive).
On a critical note, rigid adherence to the RA may lead to an
**under-utilization of resources** . In contrast to OMEs, vehicles
are resource-rich systems of their own. Thus, we recommend
promoting the physical twin to a first-class entity in automotive
DT architectures. This will allow for deploying FEs on the
vehicle, potentially in a flexible and dynamic fashion. For
example, our case study deploys the Data Pre-Processing FE
and the Actuation FE onto the physical twin, as opposed to
the DT Entity as prescribed by the ISO 23247 RA. Flexibility
in the deployment of FEs is at the heart of existing automotive
standards—e.g., AUTOSAR, the _de facto_ automotive software
architecture standard—where SW components are defined in a
hardware-agnostic manner, making it easier to retrofit existing
automotive architectures into automotive DT architectures.

Our case study further suggests that the RA is **insufficient for**
**safety-critical applications such as automotive** . Therefore,
we recommend safety to be promoted to a first-class citizen
in automotive DT standards by relying on existing automotive
safety standards—e.g., ISO 26262 [38].
Our work is a first look into the benefits and challenges of
adopting a standardized DT architecture for automotive DTs.
In order to aid the development of proper domain-specific
standards, we call for additional research into the topic, with
a particular emphasis on safety, security, data management,
and considering the regulated nature of the automotive domain, compatibility with existing standards. Additionally, we
encourage the development of automotive DT case studies and
exemplars to provide empirical evidence for further research.
In future work, we plan to research the safety aspects of
automotive DTs in more detail and elicit recommendations
